title: Dog Swallowed a Zinc Penny: Triage Guide & Next Steps
meta_desc: Learn what to do if your dog swallowed a zinc penny. Find information on zinc toxicity risks, urgency levels, and how to help your veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: Swallowing a post-1982 penny is a time-sensitive medical concern for dogs because stomach acid dissolves the zinc, which can lead to organ damage and red blood cell destruction. Seek veterinary care within a few hours to prevent serious complications.

What is zinc toxicity in dogs after swallowing a penny?

Pennies minted in the United States after 1982 are composed of approximately 97.5% zinc with a thin copper coating. When a dog ingests one of these coins, the acidic environment of the stomach causes the zinc to dissolve and enter the bloodstream. This can lead to a condition called zinc toxicosis, which may cause the destruction of red blood cells and damage to vital organs like the liver and kidneys.

Is it an emergency if my dog swallows a penny?

The urgency level for a dog swallowing a zinc penny is Medium. While it may not require a 'lights and sirens' emergency response in the first few minutes, it is a time-sensitive situation that requires veterinary attention within the next few hours. Because the coin begins to break down as soon as it hits the stomach acid, waiting until your dog feels sick could result in more severe complications.

How does a photo help my veterinarian triage a swallowed penny?

  • Share photos of the swallowed object or similar coins with your veterinary team immediately to help determine if the coin contains toxic levels of zinc.
  • Visual confirmation allows staff to distinguish between high-zinc pennies (post-1982) and older copper pennies.
  • This information helps the medical team prepare the correct diagnostic tools, such as X-ray or endoscopy, to locate and remove the object quickly.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following stabilization with fluids, oxygen, and blood products as needed, prompt removal of the zinc source is paramount, often requiring surgery or endoscopy. Emesis may be considered. A well-known source of zinc toxicity is the ingestion of USA Lincoln pennies, particularly those minted after 1982, as they are composed of 97.5% zinc (approximately 2,440 mg of elemental zinc per coin). The low pH of the stomach causes the release of free zinc, which forms soluble, caustic zinc salts. These salts are absorbed in the duodenum and distributed to the liver, kidneys, prostate, muscles, bones, and pancreas, exerting direct irritant and corrosive effects on tissues, interfering with the metabolism of copper, calcium, and iron, and inhibiting erythrocyte production and function. Zinc levels can be measured in blood to confirm toxicosis, with normal serum zinc levels in dogs and cats ranging from 0.7-2 mcg/mL, using heparinized, trace element, or EDTA tubes for sample collection.

Chapter: Toxicology, Hematology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3089)

faqs.question: Which pennies contain high levels of zinc?
faqs.answer: United States pennies minted from 1983 to the present are primarily made of zinc. Some pennies minted in 1982 also contain zinc.

faqs.question: What symptoms should I look for if my dog swallowed a coin?
faqs.answer: Common early signs include vomiting, loss of appetite, and lethargy. More severe signs of zinc toxicity include pale or yellow gums and orange-tinged urine.

faqs.question: Will my dog need surgery to remove the penny?
faqs.answer: In many cases, if the coin is caught early, a veterinarian can induce vomiting or use an endoscope to retrieve it. If the coin has moved further into the digestive tract or is causing toxicity, surgery may be necessary.
